questions.question: What is the most common histological type of invasive breast cancer, accounting for 80–85% of cases?
questions.detail: Invasive Ductal Carcinoma is the most common, making up 80–85% of cases.

questions.question: Clinically, what is the most common presentation for Invasive Ductal Carcinoma?
questions.detail: The classic clinical story for IDC is a hard, non-mobile, painless mass.

questions.question: On mammography, what is the most common appearance of Invasive Ductal Carcinoma (IDC)?
choices: An irregular, high-density mass with spiculated margins
A round, low-density mass with popcorn calcifications
questions.detail: The most common mammographic look for IDC is an irregular, high-density mass with spiculated or indistinct margins.

questions.question: Which type of calcification is most commonly associated with Invasive Ductal Carcinoma?
questions.detail: IDC is frequently associated with pleomorphic calcifications.

questions.question: On ultrasound, Invasive Ductal Carcinoma is typically described as:
choices: A parallel hyperechoic mass with enhancement
An anti-parallel shadowing mass with an echogenic halo
A mass with a central hyperechoic band
questions.detail: IDC classically appears as an anti-parallel shadowing mass with an echogenic halo on ultrasound.

questions.question: Which subtype of Invasive Ductal Carcinoma is most likely to be associated with a radial scar?
questions.detail: Tubular carcinoma is frequently associated with a radial scar.

questions.question: Tubular carcinoma is generally characterized as:
choices: A fast-growing mass with a poor prognosis
A small, spiculated, slow-growing mass with a favorable prognosis
A large cystic and solid mass in non-white patients
A mass associated with BRCA2 mutations
questions.detail: Tubular carcinoma is a small, spiculated, slow-growing mass with a favorable prognosis.

questions.question: If a patient is diagnosed with tubular carcinoma in one breast, how likely is it that the contralateral breast will also have cancer?
questions.detail: The contralateral breast will have cancer 10–15% of the time in patients with tubular carcinoma.

questions.question: Which Invasive Ductal Carcinoma subtype is known for being uncommon but having better outcomes than IDC-NOS?
questions.detail: Mucinous carcinoma is uncommon but typically has better outcomes.

questions.question: What is the characteristic shape of a Mucinous (Colloid) breast cancer on imaging?
questions.detail: Mucinous tumors are typically round (or lobulated) and circumscribed.

questions.question: Which Invasive Ductal Carcinoma subtype classically occurs in younger patients (40s–50s) and is associated with the BRCA1 mutation?
questions.detail: Medullary carcinoma typically affects younger patients and is associated with BRCA1 in 25% of cases.

questions.question: A medullary carcinoma can present with which unique feature regarding lymph nodes?
choices: It never metastasizes to lymph nodes.
Axillary nodes can be large even in the absence of metastases.
It only spreads to internal mammary nodes.
questions.detail: In medullary carcinoma, axillary nodes can be large even without metastatic involvement.

questions.question: Which subtype of Invasive Ductal Carcinoma is the 2nd most common after IDC-NOS and typically favors non-white and elderly patients?
questions.detail: Papillary carcinoma is the 2nd most common IDC subtype and favors elderly, non-white patients.

questions.question: What is the typical imaging appearance of a papillary breast cancer?
choices: A small cluster of amorphous calcifications
A complex cystic and solid mass
questions.detail: Papillary carcinoma typically appears as a complex cystic and solid mass.

questions.question: 'Multifocal' breast cancer is defined as:
choices: Multiple primaries in the same quadrant (less than 4–5 cm apart)
Cancer that has metastasized to the liver
Cancer involving the skin and the nipple
questions.detail: Multifocal disease involves multiple primaries in the same quadrant, typically within the same duct system.

questions.question: 'Multicentric' breast cancer is defined as:
choices: A single large mass in the center of the breast
Multiple primaries in the same duct system
Cancer that is confined to the nipple
Cancer that involves the chest wall
questions.detail: Multicentric means multiple primaries in different quadrants.

questions.question: Synchronous bilateral breast cancer is found on MRI in what percentage of patients with known unilateral cancer?
questions.detail: MRI finds synchronous bilateral disease in 3–6% of women, while mammography finds it in 2–3%.

questions.question: Which histological type of cancer is associated with an increased risk of synchronous bilateral disease?
questions.detail: The risk of bilateral disease is increased in infiltrating lobular types and multicentric disease.

questions.question: DCIS (Ductal Carcinoma In Situ) is considered the 'earliest form of breast cancer' because it is:
questions.detail: DCIS is 'in situ' because the cancer is confined to the duct.

questions.question: Between 'comedo' and 'non-comedo' types of DCIS, which is considered more aggressive?
questions.detail: The comedo type of DCIS is more aggressive.

questions.question: What percentage of DCIS cases on core biopsy are found to have an invasive component upon surgical excision?
questions.detail: 25% of DCIS on core biopsy are found to have an invasive component at excision.

questions.question: DCIS can occasionally present as a mass without calcifications in what percentage of cases?
questions.detail: 8% of DCIS will present as a mass without calcifications.

questions.question: The most common ultrasound appearance of DCIS is a:
choices: Microlobulated, mildly hypoechoic mass with ductal extension
Parallel hyperechoic mass with intense shadowing
Spiculated mass with an echogenic halo
questions.detail: DCIS on ultrasound is most commonly a microlobulated, hypoechoic mass with ductal extension.

questions.question: Paget's disease of the breast is defined as:
choices: Invasive Lobular Carcinoma involving the skin
Carcinoma in situ of the nipple epidermis
A thrombosed vein in the breast
A normal variant of the areola
questions.detail: Paget's is a carcinoma in situ of the nipple epidermis.

questions.question: Breast Paget's disease is associated with an underlying high-grade DCIS in what percentage of cases?
questions.detail: 96% of Paget's cases have an associated high-grade DCIS.

questions.question: For a skin lesion of the nipple-areolar complex that does not resolve with topical therapy, what is the next step?
questions.detail: A wedge biopsy should be performed for persistent nipple-areolar skin lesions.

questions.question: True or False: Paget's disease involvement of the skin is considered T4 staging.
questions.detail: Paget's is NOT considered T4, and skin involvement here does not upstage the disease.

questions.question: What is the second most common type of breast cancer after IDC-NOS?
questions.detail: Invasive Lobular Carcinoma is the 2nd most common, making up 5–10% of cases.

questions.question: The loss of which cell-surface protein is a hallmark of Invasive Lobular Carcinoma (ILC) pathophysiology?
questions.detail: ILC cells lose e-cadherin, which allows them to infiltrate the breast without forming a discrete mass.

questions.question: Because ILC infiltrates like a 'spider's web,' it frequently presents as which mammographic finding?
choices: A large round mass with popcorn calcifications
Architectural distortion without a central mass ('dark star')
questions.detail: ILC often presents as architectural distortion ('dark star') rather than a discrete mass.

questions.question: 'Shrinking breast' is a mammography buzzword for which condition?
questions.detail: The 'shrinking breast' is a buzzword for ILC, as the breast fails to compress properly and appears smaller relative to the other side.

questions.question: Compared to IDC, calcifications in Invasive Lobular Carcinoma (ILC) are:
questions.detail: Calcifications are less common in ILC than in ductal cancers.

questions.question: What percentage of Invasive Lobular Carcinoma (ILC) cases are bilateral?
questions.detail: ILC is bilateral in up to 1/3 of cases.

questions.question: Inflammatory Breast Cancer is caused by:
choices: A bacterial infection from a cracked nipple
questions.detail: Inflammatory breast cancer features tumor emboli that block lymphatic drainage.

questions.question: What is the classic clinical appearance of inflammatory breast cancer?
choices: Nipple retraction with no other signs
questions.detail: The classic sign is Peau d'orange appearance.

questions.question: Cyclic breast pain that occurs in both breasts and is worse during the luteal phase:
questions.detail: Cyclical pain in both breasts is normal and does not need evaluation.

questions.question: Which of the following breast pain scenarios 'warrants an evaluation'?
choices: Pain that peaks at day 28 of the cycle
questions.detail: Focal non-cyclic pain should be evaluated.

questions.question: What is the Negative Predictive Value (NPV) of combined mammogram and ultrasound for 'focal pain'?
questions.detail: The NPV for focal pain is right around 100%.

questions.question: 'Non-focal skin thickening' or breast edema seen bilaterally on a multiple-choice test is typically the result of:
choices: Benign conditions like CHF or renal failure
questions.detail: Bilateral edema on tests is usually from benign systemic conditions like CHF.

questions.question: If a breast is red, hot, and swollen, what is the primary differential diagnosis?
questions.detail: The differential for a swollen red breast is mastitis/abscess vs. inflammatory cancer.

questions.question: How is inflammatory breast cancer classically distinguished from mastitis/abscess clinically?
choices: Inflammatory BC is much more painful.
Inflammatory BC is often NOT painful.
Inflammatory BC only occurs during breastfeeding.
questions.detail: Inflammatory breast cancer is often NOT painful, despite its appearance.

questions.question: What is the 'general rule' for a swollen red breast that does not respond to antibiotics?
choices: Perform a skin biopsy to exclude inflammatory cancer.
questions.detail: If a breast doesn't respond to antibiotics, you must biopsy the skin to rule out inflammatory cancer.

questions.question: What is the recommended first-line treatment strategy for inflammatory breast cancer?
choices: 'Cool down' with chemotherapy and/or radiation first, then surgery
questions.detail: IBC is treated by 'cooling down' with chemo/radiation before surgery.

questions.question: Milky nipple discharge is NOT suspicious for breast cancer but can be caused by:
questions.detail: Milky discharge is related to prolactinomas or thyroid issues.

questions.question: Which class of medication is known to stimulate prolactin production and cause milky discharge?
questions.detail: Meds that mess with dopamine (like antipsychotics) can stimulate prolactin.

questions.question: What is the most common cause of spontaneous nipple discharge in a pre-menopausal woman?
questions.detail: In pre-menopausal women, fibrocystic change is the top benign cause.

questions.question: What is the most common benign cause of nipple discharge in a post-menopausal woman?
questions.detail: Ductal ectasia is the most common cause post-menopause.

questions.question: What is the most common cause of bloody nipple discharge overall (90% of cases)?
questions.detail: Intraductal Papilloma is the culprit in 90% of bloody discharge cases.

questions.question: DCIS accounts for approximately what percentage of non-milky nipple discharge cases?
questions.detail: DCIS is the worrisome cause in about 10% of cases.

questions.question: On galactography, what represents the classic look for a single intraductal papilloma?
choices: A solitary filling defect near the nipple with a dilated duct
Complete absence of the ductal system
Extravasation of contrast into the fat
questions.detail: A solitary filling defect near the nipple is classic for papilloma.

questions.question: Architectural distortion (AD) is defined as:
choices: Distortion of normal architecture without a visible mass
questions.detail: AD is distortion of the parenchyma without a visible mass.

questions.question: What is the primary differential consideration for architectural distortion?
questions.detail: Summation artifact is the main differential for AD.

questions.question: How is architectural distortion (AD) distinguished from summation artifact on imaging?
choices: Summation lines radiate to a central point.
AD lines radiate to a central point; summation lines continue past each other.
Only summation is visible on two views.
Only AD disappears on spot compression.
questions.detail: In AD, lines radiate to a point; in summation, they do not.

questions.question: If you see architectural distortion (AD) with associated calcifications, the most likely diagnosis is:
questions.detail: AD + Calcifications = IDC + DCIS.

questions.question: If you see architectural distortion (AD) WITHOUT calcifications, what is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.detail: AD without calcifications is the classic look for ILC.

questions.question: What is the management 'pearl' for architectural distortion (AD)?
choices: AD always gets a biopsy, even with no ultrasound or MRI correlate.
AD is always benign if it has been there for 5 years.
Only biopsy if the patient has pain.
questions.detail: AD gets a biopsy, period.

questions.question: Is it ever appropriate to assess an area of architectural distortion (AD) as BI-RADS 3?
choices: Only if the ultrasound is negative.
Yes, if there is no central mass.
questions.detail: Never BI-RADS 3 an area of AD.

questions.question: How should a surgical scar behave over time on follow-up imaging?
choices: It should progressively get lighter and harder to see.
it should develop fine linear branching calcifications.
it should turn into a 'dark star.'
It should migrate into the axilla.
questions.detail: Surgical scars should get less dense with time.

questions.question: If a surgical scar is seen to be 'increasing' in density on follow-up, you must:
choices: Perform a biopsy to rule out recurrence.
questions.detail: If a scar is increasing in density, you have to stick a needle in it.

questions.question: A 'radial scar' is a type of architectural distortion that is pathologically:
choices: An idiopathic sclerosing lesion (not a true scar)
questions.detail: A radial scar is not a surgical scar but an idiopathic lesion.

questions.question: What is the 'next step' after finding a breast cancer to help stage the disease?
questions.detail: You must ultrasound the axilla to look for abnormal nodes.

questions.question: Abnormal nodes are found in approximately what ratio of breast cancer patients?
questions.detail: About 1 in 3 times you will find abnormal nodes.

questions.question: Unilateral axillary adenopathy should make you worry about cancer, especially if:
choices: The patient has a known cancer on that side.
questions.detail: Unilateral adenopathy on the side of a cancer is concerning.

questions.question: Which features of an axillary lymph node are considered 'abnormal' and may prompt a biopsy?
choices: Cortical thickness > 2.3–3 mm or loss of the central fatty hilum
Presence of a large fatty hilum
A total node size of 1 cm
questions.detail: Cortical thickening and loss of the fatty hilum are suspicious.

questions.question: What is the 'most specific sign' of a pathologic lymph node?
choices: Loss of the central fatty hilum
questions.detail: Loss of the fatty hilum is the most specific sign of pathology.

questions.question: For staging purposes, how are Level 1 and Level 2 axillary nodes treated?
questions.detail: Level 1 and Level 2 nodes are treated the same.

questions.question: Rotter nodes are located between the pectoralis minor and major and are treated as:
questions.detail: Rotter nodes are treated as Level 2.

questions.question: Level 3 axillary nodes are staged and treated the same as which other node group?
questions.detail: Level 3 and supraclavicular nodes are treated the same.

questions.question: Gold Therapy (chrysotherapy) used for rheumatoid arthritis long ago can show which 'Aunt Minnie' finding?
choices: Very dense calcifications within a lymph node
questions.detail: Gold therapy shows very dense (metallic) specs in nodes.

questions.question: Snowstorm appearance of a lymph node on ultrasound is a classic look for:
choices: Silicone infiltration from a leak or rupture
questions.detail: Snowstorm nodes indicate silicone adenopathy.

questions.question: Isolated metastases to the internal mammary chain nodes are uncommon (3%) and typically occur with:
questions.detail: Internal mammary involvement is seen in medial cancers.

questions.question: When biopsying an axillary lymph node, which part should you target?
questions.detail: Target the node's cortex during biopsy.

questions.question: If you have known breast cancer and are nearly certain you are dealing with a metastasis, which biopsy technique works fine for the node?
questions.detail: FNA is acceptable if there is high certainty of metastasis.

questions.question: If you see silicone in a lymph node on a patient with implants, what should you recommend next?
choices: MRI to evaluate for intracapsular rupture
questions.detail: Silicone in a node requires MRI to look for rupture.

questions.question: IHC (Immunohistochemistry) staining is needed to confirm the diagnosis of which breast malignancy?
questions.detail: IHC staining is required to confirm breast lymphoma.

questions.question: Which histological components does the male breast LACK compared to the female breast?
choices: Elongated branching ducts and proliferated lobules
questions.detail: The male breast does NOT have the branching ducts or lobules seen in women.

questions.question: Because men lack lobules, which pathologies do they NOT get?
questions.detail: Men do NOT get lobule-associated pathology.

questions.question: Gynecomastia is a non-neoplastic enlargement of which elements of the male breast?
questions.detail: Gynecomastia involves epithelial and stromal enlargement.

questions.question: Physiological gynecomastia affects what percentage of adolescent boys?
questions.detail: Approximately 50% of adolescent boys are affected.

questions.question: What is the most common pattern of gynecomastia?
questions.detail: Nodular is the most common pattern.

questions.question: The 'Nodular' pattern of gynecomastia is mammographically described as:
questions.detail: Nodular gynecomastia is flame-shaped and retroareolar.

questions.question: Which gynecomastia pattern resembles a 'branching tree' and is a chronic fibrotic pattern?
questions.detail: The dendritic pattern resembles a branching tree.

questions.question: The 'Diffuse Glandular' pattern of gynecomastia is typically seen in men receiving:
questions.detail: Diffuse glandular is seen with estrogen treatment.

questions.question: Pseudogynecomastia is defined as an increase in:
questions.detail: Pseudogynecomastia is an increase in fat tissue.

questions.question: In pseudogynecomastia, the mound of tissue will NOT be:
questions.detail: The tissue is NOT concentric to the nipple in pseudogynecomastia.

questions.question: What is the average age of a man presenting with male breast cancer?
questions.detail: The average age for male breast cancer is around 70.

questions.question: About 1 in 4 men with breast cancer have a mutation in which gene?
questions.detail: BRCA2 is the more common mutation in male breast cancer.

questions.question: Risk factors for male breast cancer include Klinefelter Syndrome, Cirrhosis, and:
questions.detail: Chronic alcoholism and cirrhosis are known risk factors.

questions.question: The classic imaging description of male breast cancer is:
questions.detail: Male breast cancer is typically eccentric but near the nipple.

questions.question: What is the 'oldest trick in the book' regarding male breast imaging?
choices: Gynecomastia looks like a scary mass on ultrasound, which is why you always start with a mammogram.
Ultrasound is always diagnostic in men.
Biopsy is never needed in men.
questions.detail: Gynecomastia looks like cancer on US; you must get a mammogram first.

questions.question: Micro-calcifications are seen in what percentage of male breast cancers?
questions.detail: 25% of male breast cancers have calcifications.

questions.question: After gynecomastia, what is the second most common palpable mass in a man?
questions.detail: Lipoma is the 2nd most common palpable mass in men.

questions.question: Which genetic syndrome approaches the female screening range for breast cancer risk in men?
questions.detail: Only Klinefelter patients reach a risk level near the screening range.

questions.question: Are males with gynecomastia from gender reassignment on hormone therapy high enough risk for screening mammograms?
questions.detail: They are not high enough risk for screening.

questions.question: For a male under age 25 with an indeterminate palpable mass, what is the most appropriate imaging?
questions.detail: Ultrasound is appropriate for symptomatic males < 25.

questions.question: For a male OLDER than 25 with an indeterminate palpable mass, what is the first appropriate imaging step?
questions.detail: Mammography is the first step for symptomatic males > 25.

questions.question: If a male's mammogram is suspicious for cancer, the next step is:
choices: Ultrasound to stage the breast and axilla
questions.detail: Ultrasound is used to stage the breast and axilla in men with suspicious mammograms.

questions.question: Bitch tits is a slang term used by the author for:
questions.detail: Pseudogynecomastia is nicknamed 'Bitch Tits'.

questions.question: Tenderness is a clinical feature most associated with which gynecomastia pattern?
questions.detail: The nodular pattern is often tender.

questions.question: True or False: You should never ultrasound a male breast before you get a mammogram.
questions.detail: Never ultrasound a male breast first, as gynecomastia is a 'cancer mimic' on US.
